description: This figure shows Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD) Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps of austenite overlaid on Image Quality (IQ) maps for an Fe-8.03Mn-6.10Al-0.4C wt% low-density duplex steel, under different post-hot-rolling cooling paths and annealing temperatures. Subplots (a), (b), (c) correspond to air-cooled (AC) samples annealed at 800 °C, 900 °C, 1000 °C respectively, while (d), (e), (f) correspond to furnace-cooled (FC) samples annealed at the same three temperatures. Red lines mark low-angle grain boundaries, black lines mark high-angle grain boundaries, with a 50 μm scale bar for all subplots. Observed trends include finer austenite grains (3.5–5.8 μm average) in AC samples compared to coarser grains (8.1–10.1 μm average) in FC samples, and decreasing low-angle grain boundary length in AC ferrite with rising annealing temperature indicating progressive recovery. FC samples exhibit banded microstructures with small fractions of martensite in austenite bands. This figure supports that post-hot-rolling air cooling plus annealing refines austenite grains, improves austenite stability, and enables transformation-induced plasticity effect for superior strength-ductility synergy, while furnace cooling leads to poorer ductility due to coarser grains and easy crack nucleation at martensite-austenite interfaces.
